rationale: The cited paper is used for more than background: the citing paper explicitly credits Ding, Grewal, and Liechty (2005) with proposing incentive-aligned conjoint and uses its empirical results (external validity gains, price sensitivity, novelty-seeking differences) to set up key hypotheses. The 2018 study repeatedly positions its research question as moving beyond prior incentive-aligned CBC evidence to examine how behavior changes as realization probability varies from 0 to 1. It also follows prior design conventions from this stream in discussing probability settings and survey structure. This indicates a direct intellectual build-on rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds directly on Ding et al. (2005) as a core prior in incentive-aligned conjoint analysis. It extends that work by testing the full probability range from hypothetical to deterministic choice and by adding eye-tracking-based process evidence and survey-design carryover effects.
